content.full_text:
FA IR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sac:ramento. CA 95814-2329 
Eric J. Haupt 
1528 Hoover Ave 
Chowchilla, CA 93610-3410 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our file No. A-12-078 
Dear Mr. Haupt: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
June 29, 2012 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it 
renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note, there are other bodies of 
law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, that may apply to your 
situation. We urge you to check with the City Attorney's office to determine whether any other 
laws are applicable in light of the facts you present. Because your letter asks several 
hypothetical questions, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance only, pursuant to 
Regulation 18329(c). Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity 
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the Act prohibit you, as a city councilrnember, from bidding on home repair and 
rehabilitation contracts where the homeowner finances the contracted work through a city­
administered loan program, and you have been designated by the city as an eligible contractor 
under the program? 
2. If you have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to certain decisions, may you 
continue to serve on the city council if you recuse yourself from voting on those decisions? 
What is the procedure for recusal? 
'-
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All !>tatutory 
rderences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 2 
1. The Act does not prohibit a public official from bidding on contracts. However, as a 
city councilmember, you would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in any city council 
decision if the decision has a' reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic 
interests in your business or customers of your business. 
2. The Act does not prohibit you from serving on the city council because of potential 
conflicts of interest. However, you must recuse yourself from governmental decisions in which 
you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as described below. 
FACTS 
You are a California-licensed general contractor who is considering running for the 
Chowchilla City Council. 
The city participates in a program (the "Program") funded by federal and state grants for 
making loans to homeowners for rehabilitation or reconstruction of their homes. The city 
receives the grants and is responsible for disbursing the funds to qualified homeowners. The city 
has hired Self Help Enterprises ("Self Help") to administer the Program. 
Once the city is awarded a grant, Self Help administers the loan application process. 
Self Help meets with the homeowner, inspects the home, and makes a determination whether the 
homeowner qualifies for the program. If the homeowner qualifies, Self Help then writes the 
work write-up and gives the qualified homeowner a list of 15 to 20 qualified contractors from 
which to select to bid on the work. The selected contractors are invited to a bid tour where they 
receive the work write-up and bid package. The contractors must attend the bid tour to bid on 
the project. The sealed bids are opened at the time determined by Self Help and are checked for 
mathematical accuracy. Self Help then contacts the homeowner with the results of the bid 
opening. In most cases, the homeowner chooses the low bidder but is not required to do so. 
After the homeowner chooses a contractor, the project goes to the city's loan committee 
for approval. The loan committee includes one council member. If the loan is not approved, the 
project is terminated. If the loan is approved, Self Help disburses the funds. 
In your follow-up letter of May 22, 2012, you identified four types of decisions involving 
the Program that could potentially come before the city council: 
(1) Annual decisions to apply for federal and state funding for the Program; 
(2) Decisions to award the administrative services contract to Self Help or other party; 


File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 3 
(3) Decisions to amend existing policies that govern loan administration and underwriting 
that are necessary to address changes in HUD guidelines, the local housing market and similar 
matters; 
(4) Decisions involving the selection of contractors for listing as qualified contractors in 
the Program. 
You indicate that the city council's interest in the process for determining contractor 
eligibility is to ensure that the selection process allows for any qualified local contractor to be 
given the opportunity to submit a bid and have the opportunity to be selected for a contract 
award by the homeowner. 
Projects managed by Self Help under the Program have accounted for approximately 15 
to 25 percent of your business over the past 15 years. 
ANALYSIS 
Question 1 - Conflict of Interest 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in any given governmental decision. 
Steps 1 and 2: Are you a public official making, participating in making, or influencing a 
governmental decision? 
As a member of the city council, you are a "public official" within the meaning of the 
Act.2 (See Section 82048.) You will be makingY participating in making, or influencing a 
governmental decision when taking part in any city council decision regarding the Program. 
2 A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or 
her position, votes on a mailer. obligates or commits his or her agency to a course of action, or enters into a 
contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87 r:JO; Regulation 18702.1.) A public offiNial 
"participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and 
without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision 
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency if. for the purpose of influencing 
the decision, the official contacts or appears before a member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. 
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.3.) 


Step 3: What are your economic interests? 
File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 4 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows: 
• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect 
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.l(a)); or in which he 
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.l(b)). 
• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest 
of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); 
Regulation 18703.3.) 
• An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or 
more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.) 
• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her 
immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 
87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Business Entity 
You have a general contractor business. Under Section 82005, the term "business entity" 
means any organization operated for profit, including a proprietorship, partnership, firm, 
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association. Because your practice is 
operated for profit, it is a business entity. You have an economic interest in the business on two 
bases. First, you are the owner of the business, in which, presumably, you have an investment of 
at least $2,000. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703(a).) Second, you hold a management 
position with the business entity. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.l(b).) 
Source of Income 
We assume that you receive annual'income of at least $500 from your business. 
Therefore, your business is a source of income to you. In addition, because you own your 
business, a source of income to your business is also a source of income to you if your business 
receives income of $500 or more from the source within 12 months prior to the governmental 
decision. 


Personal Finances 
File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 5 
A public official is considered to always have an economic_interest in his or her personal 
finances. However, a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly 
by a public official, and a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and 
liabilities of a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect investment 
interest, are not considered separate financial effects on the official's personal finances and 
would not be analyzed separately under the "personal financial effects" rule. (Regulation 
18705.S(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule does not appear to apply to your 
circumstances and we will not discuss it further. 
Step 4: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the governmental 
decision? 
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a 
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent (1) initiates 
the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or (2) is a named party 
in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. {Regulation 18704.1.) 
Decisions I - 3 
Under the facts you have provided, your business will not be initiating, nor be a named 
party or the subject of, proceedings regarding decisions to apply for federal and state funding, 
award the administrative·services contract, or amend policies governing loan administration or 
underwriting. Accordingly, under Regulation 18704.l(a), the business entity is indirectly 
involved in these decisions. 
Decision 4 
Depending on the nature of a specific decision involving the selection of contractors for 
listing as qualified contractors, your business may be directly or indirectly involved. 
If the decision is general in nature, such as establishing a process for determining which 
contractors are eligible to bid on the contracts, or setting criteria for eligibility, your business 
would not be directly involved because it would not be initiating, nor be a named party or the 
subject of the proceeding that is the subject of the decision. If, however, the decision is whether 
or not to place your business on the eligibility list, your business would be directly involved 
because your business would be the subject of the proceeding. Under Regulation 18704.1, a 
t. person is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial or revocation of a license, permit, or other entitlement to, contract with, the subject 
person. 
From the facts you have provided, it appears that any contemplated city council decisions 
will deal exclusively with the process that determines eligibility, rather than decisions to place 


File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 6 
individual contractors on the list. If that is the case, your business would be indirectly involved 
in the decisions. 
Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard? 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation 
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest 
and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the agency's decision. The 
applicable materiality standard for governmental decisions involving business entities is found in 
Regulation 18705.1. 
Decisions I - 3 
Subdivision (c) sets forth materiality standards for economic interests in business entities 
that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision, including those that are sources of 
income. (See Regulation 18705.3(b)(l).) For relatively small businesses, the financial effect of 
a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
governmental decision will increase/decrease the business entity's annual gross revenues by 
$20,000 or more in a fiscal year, result in the business entity incurring/avoiding additional 
expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $5,000 or more in a fiscal year, or 
increase/decrease the value of the business entity's assets/liabilities by $20,000 or more. 
(Regulation 18705.l(c)(4).) 
Decision 4 
As noted above, your business may be directly or indirectly involved depending on the 
specific facts of the decision. The applicable materiality standard for business entities that are 
directly involved in a governmental decision is set forth in subdivision (b). The general rule is 
that the financial effect is presumed to be material. The presumption may be rebutted by proof 
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect 
on the business entity. If the business is indirectly involved apply the test applied to Decisions 1 
- 3 above. 
Step 6: Is it reasonably f oresteable that the financial effects of the governmental decision 
on your economic interes2 will meet the applicable materiality standard? 
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foresãeable" if there is a 
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be 
certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In 
re Thomer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
.... 


File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 7 
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is 
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thomer, supra, at 198.) 
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (111 re Oglesby 
(1975) l FPPC Ops. 71 ), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that 
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official. 
However, we can make some general observations about the different decisions. For 
example, where your business is directly involved in the decision, the financial effect is deemed 
material. 
Where your business is indirectly involved, you must consider the effects on the gross 
revenues and expenses in a fiscal year and on the value of the business's assets. If there is a 
$20,000 or more effect on the business's gross revenues in a fiscal year or its asset value, the 
effect is material. In addition, the effect is considered material if the business will incur or avoid 
additional expenses by $5,000 or more in a fiscal year. So, for example: 
(1) Annual decisions to apply for federal and state funding for the Program. You noted 
the projects in question have accounted for approximately 15 to 25 percent of your business over 
the past 15 years. We do not know the size of your business, but if a decision to cease 
participation in the program will affect your business by $20,000 in a fiscal year, the effect 
would be material. 
(2) Decisions to award the administrative services contract to Self Help or other party. It 
does not appear foreseeable that this decision will materially affect your business. 
(3) Decisions to amend existing policies that govern loan administration and underwriting 
that are necessary to address changes in HUD guidelines, the local housing market, and similar 
matters. It does not appear foreseeable that this decision will materially affect your business. 
(4) Decisions involving the selection of contractors for listing as qualified contractors in 
the Program. If your business is not the one seeking listing, it does not appear foreseeable that 
this decision will materially affect your business. 
Steps 7 and 8: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation 
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably 
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental 
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(i') and l8707(a)), or if the official is 
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). You have not presented any 
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation. 


Question 2 - Recusal 
File No. A-12-078 
Page No. 8 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200, such as a city 
council member, has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or 
she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of 
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as 
discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; 
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed 
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal 
interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.S(c) and 18702.S(d) apply. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
-zr  
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:

under the program?

continue to serve on the city council if you recuse yourself from voting on those decisions? 
What is the procedure for recusal? 
'-

you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as described below.

- 3 above. 
Step 6: Is it reasonably f oresteable that the financial effects of the governmental decision 
on your economic interes2 will meet the applicable materiality standard? 
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foresãeable" if there is a

re Thomer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
....  
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is

would be material. 
(2) Decisions to award the administrative services contract to Self Help or other party. It 
does not appear foreseeable that this decision will materially affect your business. 
(3) Decisions to amend existing policies that govern loan administration and underwriting 
that are necessary to address changes in HUD guidelines, the local housing market, and similar 
matters. It does not appear foreseeable that this decision will materially affect your business.

Steps 7 and 8: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation 
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably 
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental 
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(i') and l8707(a)), or if the official is 
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). You have not presented any 
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation. 


Question 2 - Recusal  
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200, such as a city 
council member, has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or 
she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of 
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as 
discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; 
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed 
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal 
interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.S(c) and 18702.S(d) apply. 
VJ:jgl

QUESTION: 1. Does the Act prohibit you, as a city councilrnember, from bidding on home repair and 
rehabilitation contracts where the homeowner finances the contracted work through a city­
administered loan program, and you have been designated by the city as an eligible contractor 
under the program? 
2. If you have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to certain decisions, may you 
continue to serve on the city council if you recuse yourself from voting on those decisions? 
What is the procedure for recusal? 
'-

CONCLUSION: 1. The Act does not prohibit a public official from bidding on contracts. However, as a 
city councilmember, you would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in any city council 
decision if the decision has a' reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic 
interests in your business or customers of your business. 
2. The Act does not prohibit you from serving on the city council because of potential 
conflicts of interest. However, you must recuse yourself from governmental decisions in which 
you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as described below.

Haupt 1528 Hoover Ave Chowchilla, CA 93610-3410 Re: Your Request for Advice Our file No. A-12-078 Dear Mr. Haupt: (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 29, 2012 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note, there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, that may apply to your situation. We urge you to check with the City Attorney's office to determine whether any other laws are applicable in light of the facts you present. Because your letter asks several hypothetical questions, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance only, pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Act prohibit you, as a city councilrnember, from bidding on home repair and rehabilitation contracts where the homeowner finances the contracted work through a city­ administered loan program, and you have been designated by the city as an eligible contractor under the program? 2. If you have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to certain decisions, may you continue to serve on the city council if you recuse yourself from voting on those decisions? What is the procedure for recusal? '- ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All !>tatutory rderences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS File No. A-12-078 Page No. 2 1. The Act does not prohibit a public official from bidding on contracts. However, as a city councilmember, you would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in any city council decision if the decision has a' reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests in your business or customers of your business. 2. The Act does not prohibit you from serving on the city council because of potential conflicts of interest. However, you must recuse yourself from governmental decisions in which you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as described below. FACTS You are a California-licensed general contractor who is considering running for the Chowchilla City Council. The city participates in a program (the "Program") funded by federal and state grants for making loans to homeowners for rehabilitation or reconstruction of their homes. The city receives the grants and is responsible for disbursing the funds to qualified homeowners. The
